text: While policies intended to advance the goals of educational inclusion often promise transformative change, they often fail to achieve their intended objectives and produce unintended exclusion effects. Perhaps due to the highly political nature of the underlying ideologies that guide their implementation, the goals of social equity and access envisioned by proponents of the inclusive education movement are not met. Rather than achieve greater social inclusion, the unintended consequences of these efforts often result in the exclusion of certain social groups and a diminishment of the rights of minority groups, including students with disabilities. While this situation has endured for some years, it is now arising with greater frequency in the Spanish educational context, prompting the need for an urgent evaluation and reflection of current models of inclusion that are already underway at the level of educational centres and Teacher Training Colleges. This chapter examines the effects of policies and models of educational inclusion through a critical thematic analysis of the academic literature (national and international) and official policy statements on educational inclusion, mapping the different iterations of models of inclusive education that have been implemented in Spain, from their origins in the past up to the present day, and exploring the challenges and obstacles that currently affect their implementation, resulting in exclusion for students with disabilities at the pedagogical level.
